introduction.title: Vietnamese Commands & Requests
content: Vietnamese commands are usually softer than English imperatives. The bare verb works as a direct command, but is rarely used alone — it sounds blunt.
In everyday speech, commands are softened with sentence-final particles like 'đi' (go on / encouraging), 'nhé' (OK?), and 'nào' (let's). These are essential for sounding polite and natural.
For more formal politeness, use 'hãy' before the verb or 'xin' (please) at the start of the sentence. To say 'don't', use 'đừng' (covered in the Negation module).

sections.title: Direct Commands
sections.description: Forming a basic command — bare verb or with 'hãy'

notes: Placed before the verb to form a polite or formal command.
Common in writing, signs, and speeches — feels formal in casual speech.
Bare verb (without 'hãy') is also a valid command but sounds blunt without a softening particle.
Placed at the beginning of a sentence to form a polite request.
Often paired with 'vui lòng' for extra formality.
'Xin mời' = 'please (invitational)' — used to invite someone to do something.
Common in service contexts and formal speech.

sections.title: Politeness Markers
sections.description: Words that make requests polite — essential for strangers, elders, and professional contexts

notes: Literally 'happily, willingly' — the formal/professional 'please'.
Placed before the verb.
Common on signs, announcements, and customer service.
Literally 'do a favor' — the everyday conversational 'please'.
Can go before or after the verb.
Less formal than 'vui lòng', more polite than bare command.

sections.title: Softening Particles
sections.description: Sentence-final particles that make commands sound natural and polite

notes: Placed at the end of the verb phrase to encourage the listener.
Like English 'come on, just do it' — friendly nudge.
Different from 'đi' meaning 'to go' — context decides.
The most common softener — turns commands into friendly requests.
Sounds like adding 'OK?' or 'will you?' at the end.
Essential for polite speech — without it, commands feel cold.
Used to invite shared action — 'let's go!', 'come on!'.
Often used to rally or hurry someone along.
More energetic than 'đi'.
Adds urgency to a command.
Stronger push than 'đi' — implies 'speed up' or 'get going'.
Literally means 'up' — the urgency sense is idiomatic.
Asks someone to pause or hold on — 'wait a moment before continuing'.
Common way to interrupt or ask for a moment.
